text: The surgeon begins dissection from the right common iliac artery towards the duodenum, following the aorta. The peritoneum is opened to perform a medial-to-lateral dissection, with careful protection of the hypogastric nerves visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon locates the left ureter before ligating the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) using a Hem-o-lok clip. The dissection is performed to fit the clip, ensuring the preservation of the hypogastric nerves situated anteriorly.

The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) near the duodenum or pancreas, followed by mobilization of the splenic flexure. The mesocolon is separated from the Gerota's fascia, with dissection extending laterally to the edge of the Toldt's fascia and vertically to the lower end of the pancreas. A gas point is left for further dissection of the mesentery.
The surgeon initiates upper mesodissection by separating the gastrocolic ligament, opting for a lateral-medial approach to dissect the pancreatic or colonic peritoneum.

The surgeon cuts the taut fascia at the level of the descending colon, aiming to reach the marker gas sponge. The gas sponge is visible, indicating the correct surgical plane. The splenic flexure is mobilized accurately.

The surgeon plans to address the upper rectum tumor by dissecting ten centimeters distally. The peritoneum in the medulla of placation is cut, starting with a posterior dissection while protecting the hypogastric nerves. The dissection continues from the right lateral and anterior sides. Upon initiating the left dissection, the peritoneum is opened, ensuring the left ureter is visible to prevent common ureter injuries.

The surgeon uses a linear stapler to transect the middle rectum at the ten-centimeter mark from the tumor. Initially, a green stapler is used, but a second stapler is required to complete the transection due to the rectum's size.

The surgeon identifies issues with B formation and applies a V-lock suture for support. The recto sigmoid resection is performed, extending from the upper border of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to the mesocolon. The excision is conducted extracorporeally through a 7 to 8 cm fine steel incision. A 31 mm wide anvil is placed into the colon.

The surgeon washes out the distal rectum with povidone-iodine solution. A circular stapler is positioned at the distal rectum, and the mesentery is checked for free movement and rotation. The surgeon inspects for any tissue trapped in the stapler and performs air leak tests to verify the integrity of the anastomosis.
